1: wayyōsîpû bənê yiśrāʾēl laʿăśôt hāraʿ bəʿênê YHWH — 'And the sons of Israel again did the evil in the eyes of YHWH.' The verb yāsap (H3254, to add/do again) combined with the infinitive construct marks the resumptive formula that signals each new cycle of apostasy. hāraʿ (H7451, the evil) with the definite article suggests not just evil in general but the specific well-known evil — covenant apostasy and Canaanite worship. The Deuteronomic phrase bəʿênê YHWH (in the eyes of YHWH) frames the narrative theologically: Israel's actions are evaluated from the divine perspective, not merely by their temporal consequences.
2: wayyimkərēm YHWH bəyaḏ yāḇîn melek-kənaʿan — 'YHWH sold them into the hand of Jabin king of Canaan.' mākar (H4376, to sell/deliver over) is stark commercial language for YHWH's judicial action — Israel has broken covenant, so YHWH sells them as one disposes of property. The Deuteronomic theology of covenant curses (Deuteronomy 28:68 threatens literal resale into slavery in Egypt) is operative: apostasy reverses the Exodus. yaḏ (H3027, hand/power) as the instrument of domination appears throughout Judges: 'into the hand of' marks transfer of sovereignty. Hazor (ḥāṣôr) was the major Canaanite city Josh had already burned (Joshua 11:11) — its re-emergence shows the conquest's incompleteness when Israel fails to maintain covenant.
3: wayyiṣʿaqû bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH — 'and the sons of Israel cried out to YHWH.' ṣāʿaq (H6817, to cry out in distress) is the technical term for Israel's prayer that triggers the judge cycle — it is the same verb used in Exodus 2:23 when Israel cried out from Egyptian bondage. The pattern is deliberate: every judge cycle re-enacts a miniature exodus. Nine hundred iron chariots (rekeb-barzel) represent the technological superiority of Canaan — iron chariotry gave a decisive military advantage in the plains that Israel, as a highland people, could not match by conventional means.
4: wəḏəḇôrāh ʾiššāh nəḇîʾāh ʾēšet lappîḏôt hîʾ šōpəṭāh ʾet-yiśrāʾēl bāʿēt hahîʾ — 'Now Deborah, a woman, a prophetess, wife of Lappidoth — she was judging Israel at that time.' Three designations accumulate: ʾiššāh (woman — the gender marker is deliberate, noting the unusual situation), nəḇîʾāh (H5031, prophetess — the feminine form of nāḇîʾ, one who speaks for God), and šōpəṭ (H8199, one who judges/rules). Deborah is the only judge in the book who exercises the civil-judicial function of the šōpəṭ before any military action — she holds court under the Palm of Deborah (v5). That the covenant people in this moment has a woman as their primary leader-prophet is the text's implicit critique of the men of that generation.
5: wəhîʾ yôšeḇet taḥat-tōmer dəḇôrāh — 'and she was sitting under the Palm of Deborah.' tōmer (H8560, palm tree) is a landmark; yāšaḇ (H3427, to sit/dwell) in the context of a judge's court means holding session. The designation 'Palm of Deborah' (tōmer dəḇôrāh) becomes a toponym — the judge and her court fuse into the landscape's memory. The bênê yiśrāʾēl (sons of Israel) come up to her for judgment (mišpāṭ H4941), showing her as the legitimate covenant mediator in a period when male leadership has failed.
6: wattiślah watiqqrāʾ ləḇārāq bin-ʾăḇînoʿam — 'and she sent and called for Barak son of Abinoam.' The verb sequence — šālaḥ (H7971, to send) + qārāʾ (H7121, to call) — is the commissioning formula. Deborah does not wait to be consulted; she initiates. Her command to Barak quotes a direct divine imperative: hălōʾ ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'Has not YHWH the God of Israel commanded?' She positions herself as transmitter of the divine word — the prophetic function. Tabor (har tāḇôr) as the rallying point is the prominent northeastern hill that dominates the Jezreel Valley, visible from great distances.
8: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā bārāq ʾim-tēlkî ʿimmî wəhālāktî — 'Barak said to her: If you go with me, I will go; but if you will not go with me, I will not go.' Barak's condition is sometimes read as cowardice, but his insistence on the prophet's presence is theologically coherent — the malʾak YHWH accompaniment in earlier deliverances (Exodus 14:19) ensured divine presence. Deborah IS the divine word for this campaign. His condition acknowledges that victory depends on YHWH's presence, not military strength. The consequence (v9) reframes his condition as missed honor rather than disobedience.
9: wayyōʾmer wālōk ʾēlēk ʿimmāk ʾepes kî lōʾ-tihyeh tipʾartəkā ʿal-haddereḵ ʾăšer ʾattāh hôlēk — 'She said: I will surely go with you; but the road you are going on will not lead to your glory (tipʾāret).' tipʾāret (H8597, glory/splendor/honor) is the prize that will be transferred: Sisera will fall to a woman. The statement functions as prophetic oracle delivered through irony — Barak's condition for going (the woman must come) becomes the condition for losing the honor (a woman will get the kill). kî-bəyaḏ-ʾiššāh yimkōr YHWH ʾet-sîsərāʾ — 'for YHWH will sell Sisera into the hand of a woman.' The verb mākar (H4376, to sell) is used again from v2 — just as YHWH sold Israel into Jabin's hand, now YHWH will sell Sisera into a woman's hand.
14: qûm kî zeh hayyôm ʾăšer nātan YHWH ʾet-sîsərāʾ bəyāḏekā — 'Rise, for this is the day on which YHWH has given Sisera into your hand.' The formula nātan bəyāḏ (H5414 H3027, to give into the hand) is the standard commissioning oracle for holy war; its perfect tense in the context of future action is the prophetic perfect — YHWH's deliverance is so certain it is spoken as already accomplished. hălōʾ YHWH yāṣāʾ ləpānêkā — 'Has not YHWH gone out before you?' — YHWH as divine warrior precedes the human army; the battle is YHWH's before it is Barak's.
15: wayyāhom YHWH ʾet-sîsərāʾ — 'YHWH routed (hāmam) Sisera.' hāmam (H2000, to throw into confusion, rout, destroy) is the divine warrior's signature weapon against armies: it was used at the Exodus sea (Exodus 14:24 — 'YHWH looked down on the Egyptian army and threw them into confusion'), at Gibeon (Joshua 10:10), and here. The verb does not describe what humans did but what YHWH did — 'all his army' fell by the edge of the sword, while Sisera flees on foot. The iron chariotry that made Sisera's force so formidable is rendered useless by divine intervention in the terrain (cf. the Song's reference to the flooding Kishon, Judges 5:21).
17: wəsîsərāʾ nās bəraglāyw — 'Sisera fled on foot.' The detail matters: the commander of 900 iron chariots runs on foot — his technology stripped from him. He goes to the tent of Jael (ʾōhel yāʾēl), wife of Heber the Kenite. The Kenites are descendants of Moses's father-in-law Jethro (H7073, Hobab; cf. Judges 1:16), making this a non-Israelite family with covenant loyalty to Israel. The šālôm (H7965, peace) between Jabin and the house of Heber (v17b) explains why Sisera expects safety — a diplomatic status that Jael will override through deeper loyalty.
21: wattiqqaḥ yāʾēl ʾēšet-ḥeḇer ʾet-yəṯaḏ hāʾōhel — 'Jael the wife of Heber took the tent peg (yəṯaḏ hāʾōhel).' yāṯēd (H3489, tent peg/stake) and hallāmûṯ (H4718, mallet/hammer — also H7151 maqqebet in some traditions) are the tools of the nomadic woman's craft — she knows these implements because she is responsible for pitching the tent. Jael uses her domestic competence as a weapon. The Song of Deborah (Judges 5:26) elaborates: 'her hand to the tent peg, her right hand to the workmen's mallet.' wayyiṣnaḥ wayyiqqōḇ — she drove it through his temples into the ground (hāʾāreṣ); he was in a deep sleep (niḏdəm, H7290, to be in deep sleep/stunned) and died.
22: wayyāḇōʾ ḇārāq rōdēp ʾet-sîsərāʾ — 'And Barak came, pursuing Sisera.' The irony is complete: Barak arrives to find the work done — not by his hand but by Jael's. wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā yāʾēl wayyarʾēhû ʾet-sîsərāʾ pāḡûl — 'Jael came out to meet him and said to him: Come and I will show you the man you are seeking. He went to her, and there lay Sisera dead.' The root pāgal is rare (H6299 region is not the right number — should be H4191 mēt = dead). Deborah's prophecy (v9) is now fulfillment: glory has gone to a woman's hand.
23: kēn kānaʿ ʾĕlōhîm bayyôm hahûʾ ʾet yāḇîn melek kənaʿan lipnê bənê yiśrāʾēl — 'Thus on that day God humbled (kānaʿ) Jabin king of Canaan before the sons of Israel.' kānaʿ (H3665, to be brought low, subdued, humbled) is the verb of covenant victory: when Israel is faithful, YHWH subdues their enemies; when Israel apostasizes, Israel itself is subdued (kānaʿ in Judges 3:30; Judges 11:33; cf. 2 Chronicles 7:14 where YHWH promises to heal the land if the people kānaʿû, humble themselves). The divine name used here is ʾĕlōhîm (generic God) rather than YHWH — possibly reflecting the narrator's summary perspective.
7: wəmāšaktî ʾēlêkā ʾel-naḥal qîšôn ʾet-sîsərāʾ śar-ṣəḇāʾ yāḇîn — 'I will draw (māšak) to you, to the Kishon river, Sisera the commander of Jabin's army.' māšak (H4900, to draw/drag/lead) is used here of divine military strategy: YHWH will lure the enemy to the terrain of his choosing. The Kishon riverbed plain, which floods when rains come, is precisely where iron chariots become useless. YHWH designs the battlefield. ûnəṯattîhû bəyāḏekā — 'and I will give him into your hand' — the prophetic oracle announces the victory in advance, making Barak's obedience a matter of trusting the divine word over present appearances.
10: wayyazʿēq bārāq ʾet-zəḇûlun wəʾet-naptālî qāḏəšāh — 'Barak called out Zebulun and Naphtali to Kedesh.' zāʿaq (H2199, to call out/muster — the same root as the cry of distress) here takes on its military connotation: the assembly call. Ten thousand men went up at his feet (bəraglāyw — at his heels, a military idiom for following in formation). Zebulun and Naphtali are the tribes of the northern heartland around Galilee — the territory most directly under Jabin's oppression (Hazor was in Naphtali territory). Deborah went up with him, completing the oracle's precondition.
11: wəḥeḇer haqqênî niprāḏ miqayin — 'Now Heber the Kenite had separated from Midian.' pāraḏ (H6504, to separate/divide) — the notice of Heber's separation from his kinsmen and encampment at the Elon-bezaanannim near Kedesh is a planted narrative detail: it explains how Sisera knows where to flee for safety. qênî (H7017, Kenite) — the Kenites were the clan of Moses's father-in-law (Judges 1:16), carrying a history of covenant loyalty to Israel; yet here their loyalty is complicated by Heber's peace with Jabin (v17). The geographical detail (ʾēlôn bəṣaʿananîm, the oak of Zaanannim) anchors the story: this specific tree will be the site of Sisera's death.
12: wayyaggîḏû ləsîsərāʾ kî ʿālāh bārāq bin-ʾăḇînoʿam har-tāḇôr — 'They told Sisera that Barak son of Abinoam had gone up to Mount Tabor.' Intelligence reaches Sisera through his network — the verb nāgaḏ (H5046, to tell/declare/report) is the standard term for intelligence transmission. Sisera responds immediately (v13) by mobilizing all his chariots. The enemy's swift response is not a complication but part of the divine plan: YHWH has drawn (māšak, v7) Sisera to the appointed ground.
13: wayyizʿōq sîsərāʾ ʾet-kol-rikbô — 'Sisera called out all his chariots.' zāʿaq (H2199) — the same muster verb Barak used (v10) — now used of the enemy's mobilization. Nine hundred iron chariots (teša meʾôt rekeb barzel) represent the most sophisticated military technology of the Late Bronze Age. Iron-tipped chariot wheels and reinforced chassis gave Canaanite armies devastating speed and shock power in open terrain. miḥărōšet haggôyim — Harosheth-hagoyim ('forested place of the peoples') was Sisera's base in the Jezreel plain, positioned to control the pass between the plain and the coast.
16: ûḇārāq rāḏap ʾaḥărê hārekeb wəʾaḥărê heḥāyil ʿaḏ-ḥărōšet haggôyim — 'Barak pursued the chariots and the army to Harosheth-hagoyim.' The pursuit extends to the enemy's home base — a total rout. wayyippōl kol-maḥănēh sîsərāʾ ləpî-ḥereb lōʾ-nišʾar ʿaḏ-ʾeḥāḏ — 'the whole army of Sisera fell by the edge of the sword; not one was left.' The formula lōʾ nišʾar ʿaḏ ʾeḥāḏ (H7604, not one remained) is the language of complete divine judgment — used at the Red Sea (Exodus 14:28) and in Joshua's conquests (Joshua 10:28-40). The chariot force that had terrorized Israel for twenty years is annihilated in a day.
18: watteṣēʾ yāʾēl liqrāʾt sîsərāʾ wattiʾmer ʾēlāyw sûr ʾăḏōnî sûr ʾēlay ʾal-tîrāʾ — 'Jael went out to meet Sisera and said to him: Turn in, my lord, turn in to me; do not fear.' sûr (H5493, to turn aside/depart from the road) — her invitation is urgent and repeated: turn aside, come in. The threefold hospitality gesture that follows — going out to meet him, inviting him in, covering him — mirrors the host-welcome ritual of the ancient Near East. wayyāsar ʾēlêhā hāʾōhəlāh wattəkasēhû baśśəmîkāh — he turned in to her, into the tent; she covered him with a rug (śəmîkāh H8063, a rug/cover — hapax legomenon). Her hospitality is genuine enough to disarm his suspicion.
19: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā hašqînî-nāʾ məʿaṭ-mayim kî ṣāmēʾtî — 'He said to her: Please give me a little water to drink, for I am thirsty.' The narrative is economical and ironic. Sisera asks for water (mayim) — the most basic need. Jael gives him milk (ḥālāḇ H2461, milk/cream — cf. 5:25 where the Song specifies a lordly bowl of curds). ḥēmāt heḥālāḇ (skin of milk/cream) — the nomadic woman's hospitality upgraded: she gives better than was asked. The excess of hospitality deepens his vulnerability and his sleep.
20: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā ʿămōḏ petaḥ hāʾōhel wəhāyāh ʾim-ʾîš yāḇōʾ ûšəʾēlēk wəʾāmar hăyēš-pōh ʾîš wəʾāmartə lōʾ — 'He said to her: Stand at the entrance of the tent, and if any man comes and asks you, saying "Is anyone here?" say "No."' Sisera attempts to use Jael as his lookout — to make her complicit in his concealment. The irony is complete: the man who commanded nine hundred chariots now places his security in a woman's word. She will speak a word, but not to protect him. The command to deny his presence sets up the narrative reversal — not Sisera but he himself is hiding.
24: wattelek yaḏ bənê yiśrāʾēl hālôk wəqāšāh ʿal yāḇîn melek kənaʿan ʿaḏ ʾăšer hikrîtû ʾet yāḇîn melek kənaʿan — 'The hand of Israel pressed harder and harder against Jabin the king of Canaan, until they had destroyed Jabin king of Canaan.' yāḏ (H3027, hand) — the narrative closes with the political consequence: just as Jabin's yaḏ had oppressed Israel (vv2-3), now Israel's yaḏ presses down on Jabin until destruction. hālôk wəqāšāh (going and growing harsh — an infinitive absolute construction expressing progressive intensification) describes a sustained campaign that ended only with total elimination. Jabin's dynasty that survived Joshua's burning of Hazor (Joshua 11:11) is now permanently ended.
